name: Credit Stacking
stage: Best for founders with strong personal credit
summary: A personal-credit-led strategy used to assemble multiple credit lines or 0% cards to create startup runway when the business is too early for conventional lending.
headline: Useful when personal credit is strong and the founder is disciplined enough to manage it.
explainer: Credit stacking is not magic and it is not free money. It can create flexible working capital early, but it shifts risk onto the founder and demands tight balance management.
fitSignals: Personal credit is strong and recent utilization is controlled.
The business has a specific use of funds with a payoff timeline.
The founder understands repayment pressure and cash discipline.
advantages: Can be one of the fastest early-stage runway tools.
Useful when the business has potential but thin operating history.
Can support inventory, launch costs, and selective growth moves.
risks: Personal exposure is real and easy to underestimate.
High utilization can reduce future flexibility.
Bad use-of-funds planning turns this into expensive chaos.
bankabilityNote: May buy time and operating power, but does not replace business maturity.
useCases: Launching with a tight, time-bound operating plan
Funding initial inventory or equipment carefully

name: Revenue-Based Financing
stage: Best for startups with real sales momentum
summary: A financing path tied to revenue performance, often used by ecommerce or service businesses with consistent deposits, processor data, and a clear growth use case.
headline: Works better when revenue is real, visible, and steady enough to support repayment.
explainer: Revenue-based financing can be a fit for founders with actual traction who need speed, but it requires sober math. It is not a cure for weak margins or unstable cash flow.
fitSignals: Consistent revenue and processor or bank statement history
A use of funds connected to measurable growth
Gross margin strong enough to absorb repayment drag
advantages: Can move faster than traditional lenders.
Often fits ecommerce and digital service businesses better than banks do.
Uses traction signals instead of waiting for years of history.
risks: Repayment pressure can choke cash flow if growth assumptions are soft.
Founders often underestimate how fast capital disappears when margins are thin.
Expensive capital used for weak offers usually compounds the problem.
bankabilityNote: Helpful for growth-stage execution, but not a substitute for financial hygiene.
useCases: Scaling inventory that already turns well
Funding acquisition with proven payback math
Bridging seasonal cash flow in a predictable business

name: Microloans + Grants
stage: Best for very early founders and community-backed ventures
summary: Smaller-check funding paths that can work earlier than banks for founders with limited history, especially when paired with local programs, incubators, or mission-aligned support.
headline: Lower-check, slower-moving paths that can be more realistic than headline-grabbing capital.
explainer: Microloans and grants rarely feel glamorous, but they often fit founders who need credibility, documentation, and breathing room more than massive capital.
fitSignals: Early-stage founder with light or inconsistent revenue
Business can document use of funds clearly
The founder is willing to navigate applications and local ecosystems
advantages: Can be more accessible than traditional lending.
Often comes with ecosystem support or education.
Helpful for building documents, discipline, and lender readiness.
risks: Check sizes may be smaller than the founder wants.
bankabilityNote: Strong for credibility building and profile development.
useCases: Pilot-stage operators who need a first capital win
Founders seeking non-dilutive or lower-pressure capital
